mnemonics.title: CIA Triangle for Databases
mnemonics.content: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability - The three pillars of database security. Remember: CIA keeps your data safe!

mnemonics.title: Database Access Flow
mnemonics.content: Identify → Authenticate → Authorize → Access → Audit. Follow this order: Know WHO, Verify WHO, Check WHAT, Allow ACCESS, Record IT.

mnemonics.title: Security Layers (Defense in Depth)
mnemonics.content: Perimeter → Network → Database → Table → Row → Column. Think of it like a castle: moat → walls → keep → vault → chest → lockbox.

mnemonics.title: SQL Injection Prevention
mnemonics.content: Never trust user input: Validate, Sanitize, Parameterize. Repeat: VSP - Very Smart Protection!

acronyms.usage: Real-time tracking of all database queries and transactions for security violations.
Software that enables creation, maintenance, and access to databases - must be secured.
Standard language for managing relational databases; vulnerable to injection attacks if not secured.
Encryption of data files at rest without application code changes.
Access management based on user roles within an organization.
Security feature that creates row-level security policies.

visualTips.visualization: Draw concentric circles: Outer = Network/Firewall, Middle = DBMS, Inner = Tables/Columns, Core = Data. Each layer needs its own lock.
Create a grid: Rows = Users/Roles, Columns = Objects (DBs, Tables), Cells = Permissions (Read, Write, Delete). Color-code: Green=Allow, Red=Denied.

visualTips.concept: Encryption at Rest vs in Transit
visualTips.visualization: Draw a database icon. Show a lock icon on the storage (at rest) and a shield with arrows (in transit). Both need encryption!
Timeline showing: User Action → Query Executed → Result Returned → Logged with Timestamp → Alert Triggered (if violation).

analogies.analogy: Databases are like office buildings: Authentication = Reception desk checking IDs, Authorization = Key cards for specific floors/rooms, Encryption = Safes within the building, Auditing = Security cameras recording everything.
Imagine each employee can only see over their own cubicle wall - row-level security works the same way, keeping each user in their data 'workspace'.

analogies.concept: SQL Injection as a Trojan Horse
analogies.analogy: Just like Greeks hid soldiers inside a wooden horse, attackers hide malicious SQL code inside seemingly harmless input fields to attack from within.
You hope you never need it, but without backups (insurance), a disaster (breach/data loss) becomes catastrophic. Test your backups regularly!
